title: Origins of Christianity
summary: Christianity began as a Jewish movement in 1st-century Palestine, centered on Jesus of Nazareth and the belief that he was the crucified and risen Messiah. From this small group of Jewish disciples, it grew into a diverse, increasingly Gentile faith that spread across the Roman Empire and developed its own scriptures, institutions, and identity.

concepts.title: Second Temple Judaism
concepts.summary: Christianity did not appear out of nowhere. It emerged from the religious world of Second Temple Judaism — the form of Judaism that existed from the rebuilding of the Temple in Jerusalem around 515 BCE until its destruction by the Romans in 70 CE. This was the world of Jesus and his earliest followers.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: Christianity did not appear out of nowhere. It emerged from a specific religious world — the Judaism of first-century Palestine — and it cannot be understood without first understanding that world. The form of Judaism that existed from the rebuilding of the Temple in Jerusalem around 515 BCE until its destruction by Rome in 70 CE is what scholars call Second Temple Judaism, and it was the religious, cultural, and political environment into which Jesus of Nazareth was born, in which he lived and taught, and from which his earliest followers came. To read the Gospels or the letters of Paul without understanding Second Temple Judaism is like trying to understand a conversation by hearing only one side of it. The questions Jesus addressed, the language he used, the hopes he invoked, and the conflicts he provoked all make sense only against the backdrop of this world.

The political context of Second Temple Judaism was one of almost unrelenting foreign domination. After the return from Babylon, the Jewish community in the land of Israel had lived under Persian rule, then under the Hellenistic kingdoms that succeeded Alexander the Great's empire — first the Egyptian Ptolemies, then the Syrian Seleucids — and finally under Rome. Each of these imperial powers had, in different ways and to different degrees, imposed foreign culture, foreign law, and foreign religious expectations on a people whose entire identity was built around loyalty to their own God and their own law. The Maccabean revolt of the second century BCE — a Jewish uprising against the Seleucid ruler Antiochus IV Epiphanes, who had desecrated the Temple and attempted to suppress Jewish practice — had produced a brief period of Jewish independence under the Hasmonean dynasty, but that independence had ended in the first century BCE when Rome's reach extended to the eastern Mediterranean. By the time of Jesus, Judea was under direct Roman administration, governed by prefects who answered to Rome and who maintained order with the threat and occasional reality of violence.

sections.title: Key players
body: Within this politically charged environment, Jewish religious life was rich, diverse, and fiercely contested. Several distinct groups or movements, each with its own understanding of what faithfulness to God required under foreign occupation, shaped the landscape in which Jesus appeared.

The Pharisees were the group most concerned with extending the Torah's demands into every area of daily life. They developed what they called the oral law — a body of interpretive traditions that elaborated on the written Torah and applied it to situations the text did not explicitly address. They believed in the resurrection of the dead at the end of days, in the existence of angels and spirits, and in God's ongoing involvement in human history. Despite their reputation in the Gospels, where they often appear as Jesus's opponents, the Pharisees were genuinely popular with ordinary Jewish people, and their approach to Torah interpretation is the direct ancestor of the rabbinic Judaism that would emerge after the Temple's destruction.

The Sadducees were drawn primarily from the priestly aristocracy and the wealthy elite. They accepted only the written Torah as authoritative and rejected the oral law, the belief in resurrection, and much of the apocalyptic speculation that characterized other Jewish groups. Their power was tied to the Temple and its institutions, and their interests were closely aligned with maintaining the kind of stability that Roman rule required. When the Temple was destroyed in 70 CE, the Sadducees essentially ceased to exist as a movement — they had no theological resources to survive the loss of the institution around which their entire religious world was organized.

The Essenes were a separatist movement that had withdrawn from the Temple establishment entirely, which they regarded as corrupt. They lived in communities of intense religious discipline, practiced communal ownership of property, observed strict purity regulations, and awaited what they believed was an imminent cosmic intervention by God. The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered at Qumran beginning in 1947, are almost certainly the library of an Essene community, and they have transformed scholarly understanding of the diversity and intensity of Jewish religious thought in this period.

sections.title: What happened
body: What united these diverse groups, despite their many differences, was a shared set of convictions about who they were and what they were waiting for. They worshipped the one God of Israel. They regarded the Torah as God's definitive instruction for human life. They revered the Temple in Jerusalem as the place where God's presence was most fully accessible on earth. And many of them — in different ways, with different timelines and different expectations — lived in hope that God would act decisively in history to vindicate Israel, judge its oppressors, and establish divine rule over the world. This hope, variously expressed, is what historians call Jewish eschatology — a word derived from the Greek for 'last things' — and it was one of the most powerful forces in first-century Jewish life.

The specific form that eschatological hope took in many Jewish circles of this period was apocalyptic. Apocalyptic literature — a genre of visionary writing that claimed to reveal hidden heavenly realities and the shape of the coming divine intervention — was enormously popular. These texts depicted heavenly courts, angelic messengers, great cosmic battles between good and evil, and a coming judgment in which the righteous would be vindicated and the wicked destroyed. Daniel, one of the later books of the Hebrew Bible, is a classic example of this genre. So are many texts found among the Dead Sea Scrolls and other writings from this period that did not make it into the canonical Bible. The figure of a 'Son of Man' — a heavenly figure associated with divine judgment and authority — appears in several of these texts and would become significant in early Christian interpretation of Jesus.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The practical texture of Jewish religious life in this period revolved around three institutions: the Temple, the synagogue, and the home. The Temple in Jerusalem was the only place where certain sacrifices could be performed, and pilgrimage to the Temple for the major festivals — particularly Passover, which commemorated the Exodus from Egypt — was one of the most significant religious acts a Jewish person could undertake. It was during a Passover pilgrimage that Jesus would make his final journey to Jerusalem. The synagogue had developed during and after the Babylonian exile as a local institution for prayer, scripture reading, and communal teaching, and by the first century CE it was the primary setting for Jewish religious life outside Jerusalem. Jesus conducted much of his ministry in synagogues, and his followers would use the synagogue as a base for their own early outreach. In the home, Sabbath observance, dietary practices, and the celebration of festivals maintained Jewish identity across generations and across geographic distances.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The diversity and tension of Second Temple Judaism created precisely the conditions in which a movement like early Christianity could emerge. There was no single, monolithic Jewish orthodoxy that Jesus was departing from. There was a contested, dynamic, intensely alive religious world full of competing interpretations of the Torah, competing visions of what faithfulness required under Roman occupation, and competing expectations of what God was about to do. Jesus entered this world not as an outsider but as a participant — a Jewish teacher engaging with Jewish questions using Jewish language, addressing Jewish hopes, and provoking Jewish controversies. When his followers eventually proclaimed him as the Messiah and the risen Lord, they were drawing on categories and expectations that Second Temple Judaism had already placed in circulation. Without that world, the message of early Christianity would have been unintelligible.

sections.title: Legacy
body: This concept is the necessary foundation for everything that follows in this topic. The figure of Jesus only makes sense within the world of Second Temple Judaism. The disputes about the law, the Temple, the Sabbath, and the meaning of messiahship that run through the Gospel narratives are all internal Jewish debates, not conflicts between Judaism and something outside it. The hope that God was about to act in history, that the dead would be raised, that a new age was dawning — all of this was Jewish before it was Christian. Understanding Second Temple Judaism does not reduce Christianity to something already familiar. It makes the distinctiveness of what happened with Jesus and his followers more visible, not less.

concepts.title: Jesus of Nazareth
concepts.summary: Jesus of Nazareth is the central figure of Christianity. Most historians agree on a basic outline: he was born into a Jewish family, was baptized by John the Baptist, began a ministry of teaching and healing in Galilee, and was crucified by the Romans under Pontius Pilate around 30 CE.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: Galilee, the region in northern Palestine where Jesus spent most of his life, was a place of small villages, subsistence farming, and fishing communities organized around the Sea of Galilee. It was Jewish in population but geographically distant from Jerusalem and the Temple establishment, and it sat within the broader world of the Roman Empire in ways that were felt daily — in taxation, in the presence of Herodian client kings who ruled on Rome's behalf, and in the economic pressures that bore down hardest on the poorest people. Sepphoris and Tiberias, the two major cities of Galilee, were Hellenistic urban centers that had been built or expanded by Herod Antipas and that represented the intersection of Roman imperial culture with the Jewish population of the region. The villages around them — including Nazareth, where Jesus grew up — were home to the farmers, day laborers, fishermen, and craftsmen who constituted the majority of the population and who lived at the margins of economic security, vulnerable to bad harvests, debt, and the demands of overlapping systems of taxation from both Rome and the Temple.

It is into this specific social world — not an abstract religious landscape but a particular place with particular economic conditions and particular political pressures — that the historical Jesus must be located if he is to be understood honestly. The romanticized image of Jesus as a figure who existed somehow outside of ordinary social and economic reality dissolves when you look closely at the Gospels themselves, which are full of parables about landowners and tenants, debtors and creditors, day laborers and their wages, wealthy households and the people who serve them. These are not incidental details. They are the texture of the world Jesus was speaking to, and they shaped the content of what he said.

sections.title: Key players
body: Our primary sources for Jesus's life are the four canonical Gospels — Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — written between roughly 70 and 100 CE, several decades after his death. They are not neutral biographical records. They were written by people who believed that Jesus had been raised from the dead and who interpreted every event of his life through the lens of that conviction. This does not make them historically worthless — historians use tendentious sources all the time — but it does mean that reading them requires critical attention to the difference between what they report and what they interpret. Modern historical scholarship has developed tools for identifying, within the Gospel narratives, what is most likely to go back to the historical Jesus — generally, material that appears in multiple independent sources, that fits the Jewish context of first-century Galilee, and that would not obviously have been invented by early Christian communities to serve their own purposes.

Non-Christian sources provide limited but important external confirmation of Jesus's historical existence. The Jewish historian Josephus, writing in the late first century CE, mentions Jesus twice — once in a passage that has clearly been altered by later Christian editors but that most scholars believe contains an authentic core, and once in a brief reference to 'James the brother of Jesus who was called Christ.' The Roman historian Tacitus, writing around 116 CE, mentions that a man called Christus had been executed under Pontius Pilate during the reign of Tiberius. These references are brief and not particularly illuminating, but they confirm that Jesus was a real historical figure who was executed by Roman authority.

John the Baptist is an essential figure in the story of Jesus's public ministry. He was a prophetic figure operating in the wilderness by the Jordan River, preaching a message of urgent repentance in anticipation of God's imminent judgment and baptizing those who responded. His movement was significant enough to attract followers from across the region and to make him, according to Josephus, a figure of concern to Herod Antipas, who eventually had him imprisoned and executed. Jesus was baptized by John — an event so historically secure that scholars regard it as almost certain, partly because it would have been embarrassing for early Christians to invent a scene in which Jesus submitted to baptism by someone else — and Jesus's own ministry appears to have grown out of his initial association with John's movement, even as it developed its own distinctive character.

sections.title: What happened
body: The core of Jesus's public message, as it is presented across the Synoptic Gospels — Matthew, Mark, and Luke, which are related to each other and share much material — was the proclamation of the kingdom of God. This phrase, which appears again and again in Jesus's teaching, would have carried immediate resonance for a Jewish audience steeped in the expectations of Second Temple Judaism. The kingdom of God meant God's active reign breaking into history — not a distant afterlife destination but a present and coming reality in which the current order would be overturned, the poor would be blessed, the hungry would be filled, and the powerful would be brought down from their thrones. Jesus did not merely announce this kingdom abstractly. He enacted it — through healings, through exorcisms, through table fellowship with people who were socially marginal or religiously excluded, through a consistent pattern of treating those on the edges of respectable society as precisely the people for whom the kingdom was intended.

His teaching method was largely that of the parable — short, vivid stories drawn from the agricultural and domestic world of his audience that made their points obliquely, through comparison and narrative rather than through direct statement. The parables are among the most studied texts in the history of literature, partly because they are both accessible and genuinely difficult. They invite the listener in with their simplicity and then confront them with implications that disturb comfortable assumptions. A father runs to embrace a returning son who has wasted his inheritance. Workers hired at the end of the day receive the same pay as those who worked all day. A Samaritan — a member of a group regarded with contempt — turns out to be the one who actually helps the man beaten by robbers. The parables consistently locate grace and truth in unexpected places and consistently challenge the social and religious hierarchies that organized ordinary life.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The journey to Jerusalem that brought Jesus's ministry to its conclusion was taken during the Passover season, when the city's population swelled enormously with pilgrims from across the Jewish world. Passover commemorated the Exodus — God's liberation of Israel from Egyptian slavery — and its annual celebration carried a charged political and theological meaning under Roman occupation. The Roman authorities were always on edge during Passover, aware that the holiday's themes of liberation and divine intervention could serve as a catalyst for unrest. It was in this context that Jesus arrived in Jerusalem to what the Gospels describe as a public and enthusiastic reception, performed a symbolic act of disruption in the Temple — overturning the tables of money changers and the seats of those selling animals for sacrifice — and within days was arrested, tried before the high priest and the Sanhedrin, handed over to Pontius Pilate, and executed by crucifixion.

The charge posted above Jesus on the cross — 'King of the Jews' — indicates how Rome understood or chose to present the case against him. Crucifixion was a punishment reserved for those who threatened Roman order: slaves who revolted, rebels who challenged imperial authority, and those who claimed a kind of power that Rome refused to permit. Whatever the full complexity of the charges and the motivations of those who brought them, Rome executed Jesus as a political threat, and the manner of his execution — the most humiliating and degrading form of death in the Roman world — was designed to communicate that message as publicly as possible.

sections.title: Turning point
body: In purely human terms, the crucifixion looked like the end of a failed movement. Another Jewish teacher had been killed by Rome. His followers had scattered. The kingdom he had proclaimed had not arrived. Everything about the situation spoke of defeat and dissolution. What happened next — the claim that God had raised Jesus from the dead, the transformation of his scattered followers into a bold public movement, and the eventual spread of that movement across the Roman Empire — is the subject of the concepts that follow. But the historical Jesus himself, understood on his own terms within his own world, was a figure of extraordinary moral and religious seriousness whose teaching about the kingdom of God, whose practice of radical inclusion, and whose confrontation with the established powers of his day left an impression on his followers that his death was not sufficient to erase.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The historical Jesus is the foundation on which everything else in this topic is built, but he is also a figure who must be carefully distinguished from the Christ of later Christian theology, even though the two are inseparable in the tradition. Understanding Jesus within his Jewish context — as a teacher, healer, and proclaimer of the kingdom who was executed by Rome — is the necessary starting point for understanding what his followers made of him after his death, and why those interpretations took the forms they did. Every subsequent concept in this topic is, in one way or another, an attempt to answer the question that his life and death posed to those who had known him: who was this man, and what did his death and resurrection mean for the world?

concepts.title: Crucifixion & Resurrection Faith
concepts.summary: The execution of Jesus by crucifixion around 30 CE is one of the best-attested facts about his life. Within weeks of his death, his followers were publicly proclaiming in Jerusalem that God had raised him from the dead. This conviction — that the crucified Jesus had been vindicated and exalted by God — is the heart of earliest Christianity.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: Crucifixion was one of the most brutal instruments of social control in the ancient world. The Romans did not invent it — they borrowed it from earlier cultures — but they refined it into a system of public humiliation and prolonged agony designed not just to kill but to send an unmistakable message to anyone watching. Victims were typically flogged before being led to the execution site, where they were stripped naked and fixed to a wooden cross either by nails or by ropes, left to hang in a public place where passersby could see them. Death came slowly, through a combination of blood loss, exposure, and asphyxiation as the muscles required for breathing exhausted themselves. The body was often left on the cross after death, denied the dignity of burial, as a final statement of the state's contempt for the person it had executed. Crucifixion was not used on Roman citizens. It was reserved for slaves, for violent criminals, and for those who had challenged imperial authority — people whose deaths needed to be made into a spectacle.

For Jesus's followers, his death by crucifixion was therefore not simply a tragedy. It was a specific kind of catastrophe — a death so degrading, so public, and so associated with failure and shame that it seemed to refute everything they had hoped for. If Jesus had been the Messiah, the one through whom God was going to establish the kingdom and deliver Israel, then how could he have died like a slave and a rebel? The cross was not a symbol that first-century Jews would have associated with divine victory. It was a symbol of Roman domination and of God's apparent absence. The disciples who had followed Jesus scattered. The movement, by all ordinary measures, was finished.

sections.title: Key players
body: The claim that changed everything was not that Jesus had died heroically or that his teachings lived on in his followers. The claim that transformed a scattered group of frightened disciples into a movement that would eventually reshape the world was far more specific and far more startling: that God had raised Jesus bodily from the dead. Not that his spirit had survived, which was a common enough idea in the ancient world. Not that his memory lived on, or that his influence persisted, or that his cause would ultimately triumph. The claim was that the tomb in which he had been buried was empty, and that Jesus himself — not a ghost, not a vision, but a physical presence that could be touched and that ate and drank with his followers — had appeared to them repeatedly in the days and weeks after his death.

Paul of Tarsus is the earliest written witness to this claim, and his testimony is historically significant precisely because of when it was written. Paul's first letter to the Corinthians was composed around 54 CE — roughly twenty years after Jesus's death — and in chapter fifteen he quotes what scholars generally recognize as an even earlier formulaic tradition, possibly dating to within a few years of the crucifixion itself. The formula runs: Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures, was buried, was raised on the third day according to the scriptures, and appeared to Cephas — the Aramaic name for Peter — then to the Twelve, then to more than five hundred believers at once, then to James, then to all the apostles, and finally to Paul himself. This is not a theological reflection written generations after the fact. It is a creedal statement that Paul says he received — meaning he is passing on something that was already being transmitted as established tradition when he encountered it, which places its origin very close to the events it describes.

The women who came to Jesus's tomb on the first day of the week and found it empty are another historically significant detail. All four Gospels agree that women — and specifically Mary Magdalene — were the first witnesses to the empty tomb and among the first to encounter the risen Jesus. In a culture where women's testimony was given little legal or social weight, this is precisely the kind of detail that early Christians would not have invented if they were constructing a persuasive apologetic case. It has the quality of remembered fact rather than manufactured narrative.

sections.title: What happened
body: What the resurrection claim meant theologically was not immediately obvious, and the early Christian community spent years working out its implications. Jewish thought in this period did include belief in a future bodily resurrection — the Pharisees held this conviction, and it appears in several texts from the Second Temple period. But the resurrection that Jewish tradition anticipated was a general resurrection of all the righteous at the end of days, when God would finally judge the world and vindicate those who had been faithful. What nobody expected was an individual resurrection in the middle of history, before the general resurrection, while the rest of the dead remained in their graves. The resurrection of Jesus was not the event that Jewish expectation had been anticipating. It was something new — a single person raised ahead of the general resurrection, in a way that his followers interpreted as the first installment of the new age, the beginning of the end that had not yet fully arrived.

This interpretation shaped everything that followed. If God had raised Jesus from the dead, then God had vindicated Jesus's life and teaching and reversed the judgment that Rome and the Jerusalem authorities had made. If Jesus was raised, then death was not the final word, and the kingdom he had proclaimed was not defeated but inaugurated. If he was raised, then the question of who he was could no longer be answered in purely human terms — his resurrection required a new vocabulary, a new set of categories, to describe what God had done in him and through him. The earliest Christian confessions — 'Jesus is Lord,' 'Jesus is the Christ' — were attempts to find that vocabulary, and they drew on existing Jewish categories while pressing them beyond their previous boundaries.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The practical consequences of resurrection faith were immediate and visible. The disciples who had fled at Jesus's arrest became willing to face imprisonment, beatings, and death rather than deny that they had seen him risen. This transformation — from terror and flight to bold public proclamation — is one of the historical puzzles that any account of Christian origins has to address. People change their minds about ideas. They do not typically give their lives for something they know to be a deliberate fabrication. The early Christians were not claiming that Jesus's spirit lived on or that his memory was precious to them. They were claiming that they had seen him, spoken with him, eaten with him, and touched him after his death. Whether one accepts that claim or not, the sincerity of those making it is one of the best-attested facts about earliest Christianity.

Resurrection faith also reorganized the community's relationship to the scriptures of Israel. Texts that had been read for centuries in their own contexts — Isaiah's suffering servant passages, certain Psalms, the prophecies of a coming anointed one — were now read as pointing toward Jesus. This rereading of Israel's scriptures through the lens of Jesus's death and resurrection became one of the primary intellectual activities of early Christianity, generating an enormous body of argument and interpretation that would eventually become the New Testament.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The weekly gathering of the earliest Christian communities on the first day of the week — Sunday — rather than the traditional Jewish Sabbath reflects the centrality of the resurrection morning to their identity and their worship. It was not a minor liturgical preference. It was a statement about what had happened and what it meant: the week began again from the day of resurrection, the day that God had acted to raise Jesus and in doing so had inaugurated the new creation. Every Sunday gathering was a commemoration and a celebration of that event, and every Eucharist — the ritual meal of bread and wine instituted by Jesus at his last supper and continued by his followers — was an encounter with the risen Lord who was understood to be present at his own table.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The resurrection faith is the hinge on which the entire history of Christianity turns. Without it there is no Christianity — only a failed Jewish renewal movement whose leader was executed by Rome. With it, there is the transformation of that movement into a global religion whose central claim is that death has been defeated and that the God who raised Jesus from the dead is at work in the world to bring all things to their intended completion. Every subsequent concept in this topic — the Jerusalem community, Paul's mission, the formation of the New Testament, the emergence of Christian identity — is downstream from this single, world-altering conviction. The concepts that follow trace how that conviction spread, what it produced, and what it cost.

concepts.title: The Jerusalem Church
concepts.summary: After Jesus's death, the first community of his followers formed in Jerusalem. This group was made up almost entirely of Jews who continued to worship at the Temple, keep the Torah, and see themselves as part of Israel. What distinguished them was their conviction that God had raised Jesus from the dead and exalted him as Lord and Messiah.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: In the weeks and months after Jesus's crucifixion and the resurrection appearances that followed, his followers gathered in Jerusalem. This was not an obvious or inevitable choice. Jesus had conducted most of his ministry in Galilee, and many of his closest followers were Galileans who had followed him to Jerusalem for the Passover. After his death and resurrection they could have returned north. Instead, they stayed — or returned to the city after an initial period back in Galilee — and began doing something that had not existed before: forming a community organized around the conviction that Jesus of Nazareth was the risen Messiah and Lord, and that in him God had inaugurated the promised age of renewal.

Jerusalem was the logical place for this community to form, for reasons that were both theological and practical. Theologically, Jerusalem was the center of Jewish religious life — the location of the Temple, the focus of pilgrimage, and the city most associated in prophetic tradition with the coming of God's kingdom. If Jesus was the Messiah and the new age had begun, Jerusalem was where that beginning needed to be proclaimed. Practically, the city was full of pilgrims from across the Jewish world during the major festivals, and those pilgrims represented a potential audience that would carry whatever they heard back to Jewish communities in Egypt, Mesopotamia, Asia Minor, and Rome. The early Jerusalem community's decision to stay and preach in the Temple courts was a strategic choice as much as a pious one.

sections.title: Key players
body: Peter — also called Simon Peter — emerges in the early chapters of the Acts of the Apostles as the primary spokesman and leader of the Jerusalem community. His trajectory from the man who denied Jesus three times on the night of his arrest to the man who stood in the Temple courts and publicly proclaimed the resurrection is one of the most dramatic personal transformations in the New Testament narrative. The Acts account presents this transformation as the work of the Holy Spirit, poured out on the community at Pentecost — the Jewish harvest festival that fell fifty days after Passover — in an experience of overwhelming divine presence that the disciples interpreted as the fulfillment of the prophet Joel's promise that God's Spirit would be poured out on all flesh. Whatever its precise nature, the Pentecost experience appears to have been the catalyst that moved the community from private gatherings to public proclamation.

James, identified in the New Testament and by the first-century Jewish historian Josephus as the brother of Jesus, became increasingly prominent as the Jerusalem community's leader over the years following the resurrection. He is a somewhat enigmatic figure — not one of the original twelve disciples, apparently skeptical of Jesus during his lifetime according to one Gospel reference, but transformed by a resurrection appearance into one of the pillars of the early church. His authority in Jerusalem appears to have been substantial and widely recognized, resting on the combination of his family connection to Jesus and his reputation for personal piety — early Christian tradition remembers him as a man of intense prayer whose knees were said to be as calloused as a camel's from his hours of kneeling.

The twelve apostles as a group — the inner circle of disciples who had been with Jesus throughout his ministry — served as the foundational witnesses of the Jerusalem community. Their authority rested on their direct experience of the historical Jesus and their witness to his resurrection, and this eyewitness quality was understood as irreplaceable. When one of the twelve, Judas Iscariot, had betrayed Jesus and subsequently died, the community moved immediately to restore the number to twelve by selecting Matthias, a decision that reflected the symbolic importance of twelve — the number of the tribes of Israel — as a statement that something was being fulfilled and reconstituted in this new community.

sections.title: What happened
body: The Jerusalem community understood itself not as the founders of a new religion but as the renewed and restored people of God within Israel. They continued to worship at the Temple, observe the Torah, and keep the festivals. What distinguished them from other Jewish groups was not a departure from Jewish practice but a specific conviction about who Jesus was and what God had done through his death and resurrection. They called themselves 'the Way' — a term that suggests a path or manner of life rather than a separate religious institution — and they understood their community as the beginning of the restoration of Israel that the prophets had promised.

The common life of the Jerusalem community, as described in the early chapters of Acts, has something of an idealized quality — the text presents them sharing possessions, meeting daily in the Temple courts, breaking bread together in homes, and devoting themselves to the apostles' teaching and to prayer. Whether or not this picture captures all the complexity of the actual community, it conveys the sense of intense mutual commitment and shared purpose that animated the movement in its earliest phase. People sold property and laid the proceeds at the apostles' feet to be distributed to anyone in need — an economic radicalism that reflected the belief that the new age had begun and that the old competitive arrangements of ordinary economic life were no longer appropriate for people living in the dawn of God's kingdom.

The community also engaged in active mission from the beginning. Peter and John healed a man at the Temple gate and drew a crowd to whom Peter preached. Their preaching, centered on the resurrection of Jesus and the call to repentance and baptism, is described as producing rapid growth — thousands of people, according to Acts, joining the community in its early days. This growth attracted opposition. The Temple authorities, who had been involved in Jesus's arrest and execution, were alarmed by the proclamation of his resurrection and by the crowds it was attracting. Peter and John were arrested, brought before the Sanhedrin, and ordered to stop speaking in Jesus's name. Their response — 'we must obey God rather than human beings' — became a watchword for Christian conscience in conflict with external authority.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The Jerusalem community mattered for reasons that extended far beyond its immediate size or influence. It was the point of origin from which every subsequent development in Christian history flowed. The apostolic witness it preserved — the memory of the historical Jesus, the interpretation of his death and resurrection, the practices of baptism and the breaking of bread — was the foundation on which everything else was built. As the community dispersed under persecution and as its members traveled to other cities, they carried that witness with them, and the communities they formed in Alexandria, Antioch, Rome, and dozens of other cities understood themselves as connected to and accountable to the original Jerusalem community in ways that gave the broader movement its sense of coherence and continuity.

The community also established patterns of common life that would prove remarkably durable. The practice of gathering on Sunday for prayer, scripture reading, the breaking of bread, and the apostles' teaching became the template for Christian worship that has persisted in various forms for two thousand years. The emphasis on care for the poor — widows, orphans, and those without resources were a specific concern of the Jerusalem community from the beginning — established a tradition of social solidarity that has been one of Christianity's most persistent and distinctive features. And the experience of being a community under pressure, navigating its relationship to the Jewish establishment and to Roman authority, modeled a kind of resilient communal identity that proved capable of surviving far greater pressures than those the Jerusalem community itself faced.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The martyrdom of Stephen, one of the seven men appointed by the apostles to manage the daily distribution of food to the community's widows, marks a significant turning point in the Jerusalem community's story. Stephen was a Hellenist — a Greek-speaking Jewish Christian whose background and perspective differed from those of the Aramaic-speaking Jerusalem community — and his preaching provoked a more fundamental challenge to Jewish institutional religion than the apostles' proclamation had. He was brought before the Sanhedrin, delivered a long speech interpreting Israel's entire history as a pattern of resistance to God's messengers, and was stoned to death — the first Christian martyr. His death triggered a wave of persecution that scattered many members of the Jerusalem community to surrounding regions, and that scattering, which looked like a catastrophe, turned out to be one of the most important mechanisms of the movement's growth. A young man named Saul of Tarsus watched over the cloaks of those who stoned Stephen. His subsequent career is the subject of the next concept.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The Jerusalem church is the bridge between the resurrection faith and the wider expansion of Christianity across the Roman world. It preserved the memory of Jesus, established the practices of Christian worship, and modeled the kind of communal life that gave the movement its social substance. It also represents the first and most direct expression of the Jewish character of Christianity — this was a community that understood itself as fully Jewish, reading the same scriptures, observing the same calendar, and worshipping the same God as every other Jewish community, while also making the specific claim that in Jesus the fulfillment of Israel's deepest hopes had arrived. The tension between that Jewish rootedness and the increasingly Gentile character of the movement as it spread is one of the central dramas of early Christian history, and it begins to become visible in the next concept on Paul and the Gentile mission.

concepts.title: Paul & the Gentile Mission
concepts.summary: Paul of Tarsus represents the explosive expansion of the Jesus movement into the Gentile world. Over several decades, Paul traveled throughout the eastern Mediterranean, founding communities of Jesus-followers in cities like Antioch, Corinth, Philippi, and Thessalonica. His core message was that in Jesus, God had opened a way for both Jews and Gentiles to be reconciled to God by faith.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The city of Antioch in Syria was the third largest city in the Roman Empire, after Rome and Alexandria, and it was there — not in Jerusalem — that the movement centered on Jesus first encountered the wider Gentile world on a significant scale. Antioch was a cosmopolitan city of perhaps half a million people, where Greek was the common language, where dozens of religious traditions competed for adherents, and where Jewish communities had long coexisted with a predominantly pagan population. When members of the Jerusalem community scattered after Stephen's martyrdom, some of them made their way to Antioch and began doing something new: preaching about Jesus not only to other Jews but to Gentiles — non-Jewish people who had no prior connection to the synagogue, the Torah, or the covenant traditions of Israel. The response was significant enough that it attracted the attention of the Jerusalem community, which sent Barnabas to investigate. Barnabas approved of what he found, stayed to help, and eventually went to Tarsus to find a man named Saul and bring him to Antioch. It was in Antioch, Acts tells us, that the followers of Jesus were first called Christians — a Greek word meaning something like 'partisans of Christ' — suggesting that their identity had become distinct enough to acquire a name of its own.

sections.title: Key players
body: Saul of Tarsus — who used the Greek version of his name, Paul, in his work among Greek-speaking communities — is the most important single figure in the history of early Christianity after Jesus himself. He was born in Tarsus, a prosperous city in what is now southern Turkey, into a Jewish family of the tribe of Benjamin, and he received a thorough education in Pharisaic Judaism, studying at some point under the renowned Jerusalem teacher Gamaliel. By his own account, he was intensely zealous for the traditions of his ancestors and regarded the movement centered on Jesus as a dangerous deviation from genuine Judaism. He participated in the persecution of the Jerusalem community — he was present at Stephen's stoning and subsequently traveled to Damascus with authorization to arrest followers of the Way there — until an experience on the road to Damascus that he describes as an encounter with the risen Jesus transformed him from persecutor to apostle.

Paul's account of this experience in his own letters is striking for its restraint. He does not describe a vision or a dream but uses the same Greek word — ōphthē, 'he appeared' — that he uses for Jesus's appearances to the other apostles. Whatever happened on the road to Damascus, Paul understood it as a genuine encounter with the risen Christ, a revelation of God's Son 'in' him rather than merely 'to' him, and as a direct commission to proclaim Jesus among the Gentiles. He did not immediately go to Jerusalem to consult with the apostles there. He went into Arabia, then returned to Damascus, and only three years later made a first visit to Jerusalem where he met Peter and James. His sense of his own apostolic authority was not derived from the Jerusalem community — it rested on his direct encounter with the risen Christ.

Barnabas, the Jerusalem community's emissary to Antioch, became Paul's first significant partner in mission and accompanied him on the first of the journeys that would take the message of Jesus across the eastern Mediterranean. Timothy, Silas, Priscilla, Aquila, and Lydia are among the many people who collaborated with Paul at various points and whose names appear in his letters as partners, coworkers, and hosts. The communities he founded — in Thessalonica, Philippi, Corinth, Ephesus, and Galatia, among others — were the primary audiences for the letters that constitute the earliest documents in the New Testament.

sections.title: What happened
body: Paul's central theological conviction, developed through his encounter with the risen Christ and worked out over years of preaching and controversy, was that in Jesus the crucified and risen Messiah, God had acted to include both Jews and Gentiles in the covenant people on the same basis — not through the observance of the Torah and the practices that marked out Jews as distinct from other peoples, but through faith in what God had done in Christ. This was a radical claim, and its implications were immediately controversial within the early Christian movement.

The controversy centered on a question that was both theological and intensely practical: did Gentile believers in Jesus need to become Jews — through circumcision, dietary observance, and full Torah practice — in order to be genuine members of the covenant people? Some Jewish Christians, whom Paul describes with considerable irritation in his letter to the Galatians, answered yes. Their reasoning was coherent: the covenant had always been with Israel, the Torah was God's instruction for the covenant people, and anyone who wanted to share in God's promises needed to enter the covenant through the established means. Paul's answer was a passionate no, and his argument for it drew on his reading of the Abraham narrative in Genesis. Abraham, Paul argued, had been declared righteous by God on the basis of his faith before he was circumcised — circumcision came later, as a sign of a covenant that was already in place. This meant that the fundamental covenant relationship with God rested on faith, not on the ethnic and ritual markers that distinguished Jews from Gentiles. Gentiles who trusted in Christ were, Paul argued, Abraham's children in the most important sense, heirs of the promise that had always been intended to bless all the families of the earth.

This argument had consequences that Paul himself worked out over years of reflection. If the Torah's role in marking out a specific ethnic community as the covenant people was not the ultimate basis of relationship with God, then the Torah's function needed to be reconsidered. Paul did not conclude that the Torah was bad or that God's purposes in giving it had been mistaken. He concluded that its function had been preparatory and temporary — that it had served to maintain a distinct people through whom the Messiah would come, and that with the Messiah's arrival the era of that preparatory function had ended. Jew and Gentile alike were now to be united 'in Christ' as a single new humanity, the distinctions that the Torah had maintained no longer serving as the boundary markers of God's people.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: Paul's missionary practice reflected his theology directly. He typically began his work in a new city at the local synagogue, where he could address both Jews and the Gentiles who had been drawn to Jewish monotheism and ethics without fully converting — a group Acts calls 'God-fearers.' When he was expelled from synagogues, as frequently happened, he moved to private homes, workshops, and any other available space, continuing to form communities that included both Jews and Gentiles, enslaved people and free, men and women. His letters to these communities are the earliest Christian documents we possess, and they give us an extraordinarily direct window into both the theology and the practical struggles of the movement in its first generation.

The letters addressed a remarkable range of issues: conflicts within communities about food offered to idols, questions about marriage and sexual ethics, disputes about spiritual gifts and their proper use in worship, confusion about what would happen to community members who died before Jesus returned, and above all the persistent question of the relationship between the new communities and the Torah traditions they had either grown up with or were being pressured to adopt. Paul's responses to these questions are not always systematic or consistent — they were pastoral responses to specific situations, not academic treatises — but they contain some of the most profound theological thinking in the entire New Testament, and they have exercised an influence on Christian thought that is difficult to overstate.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The Council of Jerusalem, described in Acts 15 and referred to obliquely in Paul's letter to the Galatians, was the moment at which the early Christian movement confronted the Gentile question most directly and arrived at a provisional answer. Representatives of the Jerusalem community — including Peter, James, and Paul — gathered to debate whether Gentile believers needed to be circumcised and keep the full Torah. The decision reached, associated especially with James's leadership, was that they did not — that Gentiles should abstain from food offered to idols, from sexual immorality, from eating blood, and from meat from which the blood had not been drained, but that circumcision and full Torah observance were not required. This decision did not resolve all the tensions that the Gentile mission had created, but it was a decisive step toward the Christianity that would eventually become a majority Gentile movement — and it was a decision that Paul's missionary success had made unavoidable.

sections.title: Legacy
body: Paul's mission transformed Christianity from a Jewish renewal movement centered in Jerusalem into a trans-ethnic, geographically dispersed religion with communities across the Roman Empire. The theology he developed — justification by faith, the unity of Jew and Gentile in Christ, the life of the Spirit as the animating principle of the new community — became the dominant framework through which subsequent Christianity understood itself, particularly after the Reformation of the sixteenth century made Paul's letters the primary lens for reading the entire New Testament. His letters are also the earliest evidence we have for many of the practices and convictions of the earliest Christian communities — their worship, their ethics, their conflicts, and their hopes — and without them our knowledge of the first generation of Christian history would be vastly impoverished. The next concept follows the widening separation between the Jesus movement and Judaism as a whole — a separation that Paul both contributed to and, in some respects, tried to prevent.

concepts.title: Tensions with Judaism
concepts.summary: Christianity began as a movement within Judaism, but within a few generations it had become a largely Gentile religion increasingly distinct from its Jewish roots. This shift unfolded through a series of tensions and conflicts over questions such as: Is Jesus the Messiah? Must Gentile believers keep the Jewish law? How should followers of Jesus relate to the Temple and the wider Jewish community?

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: In the earliest years of the Jesus movement, the boundary between it and the rest of Judaism was unclear — to outsiders and, in many ways, to the participants themselves. The first followers of Jesus were Jews who continued to worship in the Temple, observe the Sabbath, read the same scriptures, and understand themselves as belonging to the people of Israel. What distinguished them was a specific conviction about Jesus — that he was the risen Messiah — not a departure from Jewish identity as they understood it. The question of whether the Jesus movement was a sect within Judaism or something separate from it was not answered immediately or cleanly. It was answered gradually, through decades of controversy, conflict, and mutual definition, in a process that was painful, theologically consequential, and historically irreversible.

To understand how the separation happened, it helps to understand that the first century CE was a period of enormous flux and diversity within Judaism itself. The destruction of the Temple by Rome in 70 CE — which came after a catastrophic Jewish revolt against Roman rule that began in 66 CE and ended with the sack of Jerusalem and the dispersal of much of its population — was as shattering for Judaism as the Babylonian exile had been five centuries earlier. The Sadducees, whose entire religious world had been organized around the Temple and its institutions, effectively ceased to exist. The Essenes, whose community at Qumran was destroyed by Roman troops, disappeared from history. The Zealots and other revolutionary movements were crushed. What survived was primarily the Pharisaic movement, which had already developed the theological resources to sustain Jewish life without a Temple — Torah study, prayer, and synagogue community — and which became the foundation of the rabbinic Judaism that has defined Jewish life ever since. The Jesus movement survived too, but it was increasingly shaped by its Gentile majority and was developing in directions that made its relationship to emerging rabbinic Judaism ever more strained.

sections.title: Key players
body: The parting of the ways between Judaism and Christianity was not a single event with a clear date and a recognized cast of decision-makers. It was a process that played out differently in different places, at different paces, and among different communities. But certain figures and moments illuminate the fault lines most clearly.

The Jewish Christians of Jerusalem — the original community of Jewish followers of Jesus — occupied an increasingly anomalous position as the movement spread and its Gentile majority grew. They maintained their Jewish identity and their Torah observance, but they were suspected by non-Christian Jews of compromising Judaism by their acceptance of Jesus as Messiah, and they were increasingly marginalized within a broader Christian movement that was moving away from its Jewish roots. According to later tradition, the Jerusalem community fled the city before the Roman siege of 70 CE, settling in the Transjordanian town of Pella, and it never regained its earlier prominence. After the destruction of Jerusalem, the geographic and demographic center of Christianity shifted decisively toward cities like Antioch, Ephesus, and Rome, where Gentile Christians were the majority and Jewish Christian practice was a minority concern.

The rabbis who gathered in the town of Yavneh after the Temple's destruction to reconstitute Jewish religious life under Roman rule were engaged in a process of boundary definition that inevitably affected the status of Jewish Christians. The precise nature of this process is debated by historians, but its general direction is clear: emerging rabbinic Judaism defined itself in ways that made the Jesus movement's claims about Jesus incompatible with full participation in the synagogue community. A prayer called the Birkat ha-Minim — the blessing or curse against heretics — was added to the synagogue liturgy at some point in this period, and while its exact targets and its precise role in excluding Jewish Christians are disputed, it reflects the broader process by which rabbinic Judaism and the Jesus movement were defining themselves in contrast to each other.

sections.title: What happened
body: The theological content of the separation grew out of several overlapping disputes, each of which had both religious and social dimensions. The most fundamental was the question of Jesus himself: was he the Messiah? For non-Christian Jews, the answer was clearly no — not least because the Messiah was expected to bring peace, justice, and the redemption of Israel, none of which had visibly occurred. Jesus had been executed by Rome, the Temple had been destroyed, and Israel remained under foreign domination. Christian claims that the Messiah had come but had been rejected, that his kingdom was spiritual rather than political, or that his return would complete what his first coming had begun were, from a Jewish perspective, a redefinition of messiahship so radical as to empty the category of its meaning.

The question of the Torah was equally divisive. Paul's argument that Torah observance was not required for Gentile believers, and his broader theological claim that the era of the Torah as the boundary marker of God's people had ended with the coming of Christ, was directly incompatible with the rabbinic insistence that Torah observance was the defining commitment of Jewish identity and the irreplaceable framework for the covenant relationship with God. As Gentile Christianity grew and its theology of Torah was increasingly shaped by Paul's letters, the gap between the two communities' understanding of the Hebrew scriptures widened correspondingly.

The developing Christian understanding of Jesus himself — particularly the high Christological claims that emerged in the Gospel of John, where Jesus is identified with the divine Logos who was present at creation and through whom all things were made — pressed against the boundaries of Jewish monotheism in ways that the earlier, more modest formulations had not. Jews who understood themselves as defending the absolute unity of God against any compromise or qualification found the Christian claim that Jesus was divine, or that God was in some sense threefold, deeply threatening to the most fundamental conviction of their tradition.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The separation of Christianity from Judaism mattered in ways that extended far beyond the two communities immediately involved. As Christianity grew and eventually became the dominant religion of the Roman Empire, the theological arguments that had been developed to justify the separation — that Christianity had superseded Judaism, that the church had replaced Israel as God's people, that the Jews' rejection of their Messiah had resulted in their rejection by God — became the ideological foundation for centuries of Christian anti-Judaism. The charge of deicide — that the Jewish people collectively bore responsibility for the death of Jesus — was used to justify discrimination, persecution, forced conversion, and eventually massacre. The theological polemic of the parting of the ways, which had begun as an internal family dispute about the interpretation of shared scriptures, became the intellectual framework for some of the most sustained and devastating persecution in human history.

This history has required serious reckoning by Christian theologians and churches in the modern era, and the Second Vatican Council's declaration Nostra Aetate in 1965 — which repudiated the charge of collective Jewish guilt for Jesus's death and affirmed the ongoing validity of God's covenant with Israel — was a landmark acknowledgment of how badly Christian theology had gone wrong in its treatment of Jews and Judaism. Jewish-Christian dialogue since then has sought to recover the Jewish roots of Christianity and to build relationships of genuine mutual respect, but the wounds of fifteen centuries of Christian anti-Judaism cannot be quickly healed.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The Bar Kokhba revolt of 132 to 135 CE — the second great Jewish revolt against Rome — was the event that most definitively sealed the separation between Judaism and Christianity. Simon Bar Kokhba, the leader of the revolt, was hailed by some as the Messiah, including the great rabbi Akiva. Jewish Christians, who could not acknowledge Bar Kokhba as Messiah because they already had one, refused to participate in the revolt. This refusal was experienced by the broader Jewish community as a betrayal at a moment of existential crisis, and it made the continued inclusion of Christians within Judaism practically impossible. After the revolt's crushing defeat, the Emperor Hadrian rebuilt Jerusalem as a pagan city called Aelia Capitolina, banned Jews from entering it, and effectively ended Jewish Christianity's connection to its geographic and cultural homeland. The separation that had been developing for decades was now, for all practical purposes, complete.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The tensions between the Jesus movement and Judaism produced something important even in their destructiveness: they forced both traditions to define themselves more clearly, to articulate their convictions more precisely, and to develop the institutional and textual resources that would sustain them through the centuries ahead. Rabbinic Judaism's emphasis on Torah study, synagogue community, and the ongoing interpretation of scripture was partly shaped by the need to maintain Jewish identity in a world where the Temple was gone and where a competing movement claimed to have fulfilled Israel's scriptures. Christianity's development of its own canon of scripture, its creeds, and its institutional structures was partly shaped by the need to define itself in relation to the Judaism from which it had emerged and from which it was separating. The two traditions are, in a real sense, siblings whose development has been shaped by their relationship to each other — a relationship that remains theologically significant and historically consequential to this day.

concepts.title: The Roman World
concepts.summary: Christianity spread and took shape within the vast, complex world of the Roman Empire. Its roads, shipping routes, and common languages made long-distance travel and communication far easier than in earlier eras. This environment created both opportunities and dangers for the early Christian movement.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: To understand how a small Jewish movement that began in a provincial corner of the Roman Empire became, within three centuries, the official religion of that same empire, you have to understand the world through which it traveled. The Roman Empire of the first three centuries CE was the largest and most complex political entity the Mediterranean world had ever seen. At its height it stretched from Britain in the northwest to Mesopotamia in the east, from the Rhine and Danube rivers in the north to the Sahara Desert in the south, encompassing a population of perhaps fifty to sixty million people speaking dozens of languages, worshipping hundreds of deities, and organized into an enormous diversity of local cultures and traditions. What held this diversity together was a set of shared institutions: Roman law, Roman roads, Roman currency, the Latin and Greek languages as common media of communication, and the Roman army as the ultimate guarantor of order.

This infrastructure was not created for the benefit of Christianity, but Christianity benefited from it enormously. The roads that Roman legions used to move troops and that Roman merchants used to move goods also carried missionaries, letters, and converts. The Greek language that educated people across the eastern empire used as their common tongue was the language in which the New Testament was written and in which the earliest Christian communities conducted their worship and their correspondence. The relative security of travel within the empire, enforced by Roman military power and Roman law, made journeys like Paul's missionary travels across thousands of miles of the eastern Mediterranean possible in ways that would have been far more difficult and dangerous in a more fragmented political world. The empire that would periodically persecute Christians was also, paradoxically, the infrastructure that made Christianity's spread possible.

sections.title: Key players
body: The Roman imperial system was organized around the figure of the emperor, whose authority was simultaneously political, military, and increasingly religious. The imperial cult — the worship of the emperor as a divine or semi-divine figure — was not a single unified institution but a complex set of practices that varied across the empire, including sacrifices to the emperor's genius, temples dedicated to the imperial family, and festivals celebrating imperial victories and anniversaries. Participation in these practices was not merely a religious act — it was a civic obligation, a demonstration of loyalty to the empire and its order. Jews had been granted a specific exemption from imperial cult participation because of the antiquity of their monotheistic tradition, but this exemption was specific to Jews and did not automatically extend to Christians, who were a new movement without the protection of recognized antiquity.

The specific Roman officials whose decisions most directly affected the early Christian communities were the provincial governors who had authority over criminal matters in their regions. Pontius Pilate had executed Jesus. The proconsul Gallio had dismissed the charges brought against Paul in Corinth. Pliny the Younger, governor of Bithynia in Asia Minor around 112 CE, wrote a famous letter to the Emperor Trajan asking for guidance about how to handle Christians who were being brought before him. His letter is one of the most valuable documents we have for understanding how Roman officials actually thought about Christianity in this period — with a mixture of puzzlement, pragmatic concern for public order, and a desire for clear policy that the situation seemed not yet to have produced.

The ordinary people of the empire — the craftsmen, merchants, soldiers, slaves, freedpeople, and farmers who made up its vast majority — are less visible in the historical record but no less important. It was among these people that Christianity spread most rapidly and most deeply in its earliest centuries. The movement's appeal to people across the social spectrum — its message of equal standing before God regardless of social status, its communal care for the poor and sick, its offer of meaning and belonging in a world where traditional community structures were often disrupted by the mobility and anonymity of urban life — was a significant part of its social power.

sections.title: What happened
body: The relationship between the early Christian communities and Roman authority was complex, variable, and often misrepresented by later popular accounts that imagine a continuous, empire-wide persecution from the beginning. The reality was more nuanced. For most of the first century, Rome did not clearly distinguish between Jews and Christians, and the legal protection that Jews enjoyed extended in practice to many Christian communities. The first clearly documented Roman persecution of Christians occurred under Nero in 64 CE, when he made Christians the scapegoats for the great fire of Rome — an action that appears to have been locally specific and politically motivated rather than an expression of empire-wide anti-Christian policy. The Gospels of Matthew and John and the book of Revelation all reflect communities experiencing significant conflict with Jewish synagogue authorities, but not necessarily with Roman political authority.

The situation shifted as Christianity became more clearly distinct from Judaism and as its refusal to participate in civic religious practices became more visible and more of a social problem. Christians refused to sacrifice to the emperor's genius and to the traditional gods of Rome, which from a Roman perspective made them atheists — people who denied the existence of the gods — and potential threats to the divine favor on which Rome's prosperity and military success depended. The charge was not primarily theological but social: a religion that refused to participate in the religious practices that held communities together was a potentially antisocial force, a community that set itself apart from the normal bonds of civic obligation.

Persecution, when it came, was typically local rather than empire-wide, triggered by specific incidents of social tension or by the initiatives of particular officials rather than by sustained imperial policy. The great systematic persecutions — under Decius in the 250s, Valerian in the 260s, and Diocletian in the early 300s — came relatively late in the pre-Constantinian period and reflected specific crises of imperial authority that made religious uniformity seem politically necessary. These persecutions were severe and produced significant numbers of martyrs — people who died rather than deny their faith — but they also produced the phenomenon of the lapsi, those who apostatized under pressure and then sought readmission to the community when the persecution ended, generating fierce internal debates about the nature of forgiveness and the standards of Christian membership.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The Roman world mattered for Christianity not only as the context through which it spread but as a set of intellectual and cultural resources that shaped the form Christianity eventually took. Greek philosophy — Platonic, Stoic, and later Neoplatonic — provided early Christian theologians with the conceptual vocabulary to articulate their convictions about God, Christ, and the soul in terms that educated people in the Greco-Roman world could engage with. The encounter between Christian faith and Greek philosophy produced the great tradition of patristic theology — the writings of the church fathers — which addressed questions about the nature of Christ, the relationship between the divine and human in Jesus, and the inner life of God itself in philosophical terms that the New Testament had not fully elaborated. This encounter was not always comfortable — there were Christian thinkers who regarded Greek philosophy as a corruption of pure faith — but it was unavoidable and ultimately productive, generating a theological tradition of remarkable depth and sophistication.

Roman legal culture also shaped Christianity in important ways. The Christian understanding of sin and forgiveness was partly developed in legal categories. The structure of canon law, which governed the internal life of the church, drew extensively on Roman legal precedents. The organizational forms of the early church — the diocese as an administrative unit, the bishop as the head of a geographic region corresponding roughly to Roman administrative divisions — reflected Roman governmental structures. Christianity did not simply reject the Roman world. It absorbed and transformed it, and the result was a religion that was both rooted in Jewish scripture and tradition and deeply shaped by Greco-Roman culture.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The conversion of the Emperor Constantine in 312 CE and his subsequent legalization and patronage of Christianity transformed the movement's relationship to Roman power from persecution to privilege with startling speed. Constantine's victory at the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, which he attributed to the intervention of the Christian God, led to the Edict of Milan in 313, which granted Christians legal toleration, and to a series of subsequent decisions — returning confiscated property, funding church construction, exempting clergy from civic obligations — that gave the church resources and status it had never previously had. Constantine's convening of the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE to resolve the Arian controversy over Christ's relationship to God was a landmark moment in which imperial power and theological debate intersected directly, with enormous consequences for the subsequent development of Christian doctrine.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The Roman world shaped Christianity in ways that are still visible in the twenty-first century. The organizational structures of the Catholic and Orthodox churches, the Latin of the Roman liturgy, the legal frameworks of canon law, the theological vocabulary developed by Greek-speaking theologians working within Platonic philosophical categories — all of these reflect the deep imprint of the Roman imperial world on the religion that eventually became its official faith. Understanding the Roman context does not reduce Christianity to a product of its environment — the conviction that drove its spread was theological, not sociological — but it is essential for understanding why Christianity took the specific institutional and theological forms it did, and why those forms have proven so durable across so many subsequent centuries and cultures.

concepts.title: From Oral Tradition to New Testament
concepts.summary: The first followers of Jesus did not immediately sit down to write a new Bible. For decades, the Jesus movement lived by oral tradition and by reading Israel's scriptures in a new light. The New Testament emerged gradually from this oral world, through a process of composition, circulation, and recognition that extended over roughly a century.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The New Testament did not arrive fully formed in the weeks after Jesus's resurrection. It was not dictated, compiled, or authorized at a single moment by a single authority. It was the product of a process that unfolded over roughly a century — from the earliest letters of Paul in the late 40s CE to the latest books of the collection, possibly composed as late as the early second century — and the process by which these specific twenty-seven documents came to be recognized as authoritative scripture took even longer, extending well into the fourth century. Understanding how the New Testament came to exist, and why these particular texts rather than others were eventually included in it, is essential to understanding what kind of document it is and how it should be read.

Before there was a written New Testament, the movement centered on Jesus lived and spread through oral tradition. This is not a deficiency or a problem to be explained away — it was the normal mode of cultural transmission in the ancient world, and oral tradition in cultures that practiced it carefully was far more reliable than modern people, accustomed to writing down everything that matters, tend to assume. The stories of Jesus's healing miracles, the parables he told, the disputes he had with religious authorities, the events of his final days in Jerusalem, and the resurrection appearances — all of these circulated orally in the early communities, told and retold in the context of worship, teaching, and communal formation. They were not random anecdotes. They were shaped by the purposes of the communities that preserved them — by the need to instruct new believers, to answer challenges from critics, to settle internal disputes, and to nourish the faith of those already committed — and that shaping is visible in the texts that eventually emerged from oral tradition into written form.

sections.title: Key players
body: Paul of Tarsus is the earliest writer in the New Testament, and his letters are the closest thing we have to a direct, undistorted window into the first generation of Christian experience. His letters to the Thessalonians, written around 50 CE, are probably the earliest Christian documents in existence — composed roughly twenty years after Jesus's death and before any of the Gospels. They are not systematic theological treatises. They are pastoral correspondence, written to address specific problems and questions in specific communities, and they reveal a movement that is still working out its convictions in real time, still navigating the tensions between its Jewish heritage and its increasingly Gentile membership, still waiting with genuine urgency for the return of Jesus that the earliest generation expected to happen within their own lifetimes.

The evangelists — the authors of the four canonical Gospels — were the shapers of Christian memory in its most influential form. Mark, generally regarded by scholars as the earliest Gospel, was probably written around 70 CE, the year the Temple was destroyed, and its narrative has an urgent, compressed quality — the word 'immediately' appears dozens of times — that reflects a community living in the shadow of crisis. Matthew, writing probably in the 80s CE for a community that was navigating its relationship with the emerging rabbinic Judaism of the post-Temple period, presents Jesus as the fulfillment of Israel's scriptures and as a new Moses who delivers the definitive interpretation of the Torah. Luke, writing around the same period and addressing a Gentile audience, emphasizes the universal scope of salvation and the particular concern of Jesus for the poor, the outcast, and those on the margins of respectable society. John, the latest of the four Gospels and the most theologically developed, presents Jesus in explicitly cosmic terms — as the eternal Word of God who was present at creation and through whom all things were made — and uses extended dialogues and discourses rather than the rapid-fire narrative episodes that characterize the other three.

sections.title: What happened
body: The four canonical Gospels were not the only accounts of Jesus's life and teaching that circulated in early Christianity. The Gospel of Thomas — a collection of sayings attributed to Jesus, discovered at Nag Hammadi in Egypt in 1945 — is probably the most significant of a number of non-canonical gospels, and scholars debate the extent to which it preserves authentic traditions going back to the historical Jesus. Other texts — the Gospel of Peter, the Gospel of Mary, the Gospel of Philip — circulated in various Christian communities and reflect the theological diversity of the second and third century movement. The process by which some texts came to be widely recognized as authoritative while others did not was gradual, contested, and never entirely free of the political and theological controversies that surrounded it.

The criteria that early Christians applied — sometimes explicitly, more often implicitly — in assessing which texts deserved to be treated as scripture were several. Apostolic connection mattered: texts associated with apostles or their close associates carried greater authority than texts of uncertain or recent origin. Widespread use mattered: texts that had been read in communities across the empire for generations had a kind of de facto authority that more recent or more localized texts could not claim. Theological consistency with the core proclamation mattered: texts that seemed to contradict the basic convictions about Jesus that had been established in the earliest tradition were viewed with suspicion. And orthogonality to the various forms of Gnosticism that offered alternative interpretations of Jesus mattered enormously in the second and third centuries, as church leaders sought to define a mainstream tradition against movements they regarded as distorting the original message.

The canon took shape over a long period. By the late second century, the four Gospels and most of Paul's letters were widely recognized across the major Christian communities. Debates continued about several texts that are now in the New Testament — including Hebrews, James, 2 Peter, 2 and 3 John, Jude, and Revelation — as well as about several texts that were ultimately not included, such as the Shepherd of Hermas and the Didache, which were well regarded in some communities and treated almost as scripture in others. The earliest list of the twenty-seven books that now constitute the New Testament as most Christians know it appears in a letter written by Athanasius, bishop of Alexandria, in 367 CE. Regional church councils at Hippo in 393 and Carthage in 397 confirmed the same list.
