But it is important to understand that these councils did not create the canon by fiat — they ratified what had already become the de facto practice of the majority of Christian communities across the empire.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The formation of the New Testament mattered for reasons that went beyond the specific texts included in it. The very existence of a fixed canon — a bounded set of authoritative writings — changed the character of Christian theological authority in a fundamental way. It gave the church a specific, publicly available body of texts that could serve as the norm against which teaching and practice were measured, that could be appealed to in disputes, and that connected every Christian community to the foundational events and convictions of the movement's first generation. It also, inevitably, created a boundary: the texts inside the canon had a status that texts outside it did not, and the decisions about where to draw that boundary had consequences for which voices and perspectives from early Christianity would be preserved and which would be marginalized or lost.

The New Testament's own internal diversity is one of its most important and most often overlooked features. These twenty-seven books are not a single, harmonious theological system. They reflect different communities, different historical circumstances, different theological emphases, and sometimes genuine tensions with each other. Paul's understanding of the law and of justification is not identical to Matthew's. John's Christology is more developed than Mark's. The letter of James, with its insistence that faith without works is dead, sits in a creative tension with Paul's language about justification by faith alone that has generated centuries of theological controversy. This diversity is not a problem to be resolved. It is a feature of the canon that reflects the genuine complexity of the movement's origins and invites the kind of sustained engagement and interpretation that has characterized Christian intellectual life ever since.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The Muratorian Fragment, a document usually dated to the late second century CE and discovered in the eighteenth century in Milan, contains the earliest known list of New Testament books and the earliest explicit discussion of why some books should be included in authoritative lists and others should not. Its existence demonstrates that by the late second century, the question of which texts carried apostolic authority was being actively discussed and debated, not simply assumed. The fragment accepts the four Gospels, the Acts of the Apostles, thirteen letters of Paul, Jude, and two letters of John, but excludes texts it regards as too recent or too associated with heretical movements. Its significance lies not in its specific decisions — some of which differ from the eventual canonical consensus — but in the fact that it represents the early Christian community engaging in the kind of explicit self-reflection about textual authority that would eventually produce the canon.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The New Testament that emerged from this long process of composition, circulation, and recognition has shaped the culture of the Western world in ways that extend far beyond explicitly religious life. Its narratives and images — the Good Samaritan, the Prodigal Son, the Sermon on the Mount, the feeding of the five thousand, the resurrection — have permeated literature, art, law, and moral philosophy for two thousand years. Its Greek has shaped the development of the Greek language itself. Its concepts — grace, redemption, incarnation, resurrection, apocalypse — have entered the general vocabulary of Western culture as frames for understanding human experience that extend well beyond their original theological context. The formation of the New Testament was not just a religious event. It was one of the most consequential literary and cultural events in the history of the Western world, and its effects are visible everywhere in the civilization that grew up around it.

concepts.title: From Sect to Religion
concepts.summary: By the end of the first century, the Jesus movement had spread throughout the eastern Roman Empire. Over the next two centuries, it continued to grow, diversify, and define itself, moving from a predominantly Jewish sect to a largely Gentile, distinct religion with its own institutions, canon, creeds, and identity.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: By the end of the first century CE, the movement that had begun with a small group of Jewish disciples in Jerusalem had spread to cities across the Roman Empire and beyond. There were Christian communities in Rome, Corinth, Ephesus, Antioch, Alexandria, and dozens of smaller cities and towns. These communities were diverse — in their social composition, in their theological emphases, in their liturgical practices, and in the degree to which they maintained connections to their Jewish roots. What they shared was a core conviction about Jesus, a set of founding texts that were beginning to acquire canonical status, and a network of relationships — maintained through traveling teachers, letters, and occasional gatherings of representatives — that gave the broader movement a sense of shared identity despite its geographic dispersal and internal diversity.

But being a movement is not the same as being a religion. A movement can sustain itself on conviction and momentum for a generation or two, but to survive across centuries and cultures it needs institutions — structures of leadership and accountability, mechanisms for transmitting its convictions to new generations, ways of resolving internal disputes and defining the boundaries of acceptable belief and practice. The transformation of the Jesus movement from a sect within Judaism into a distinct religion with its own institutions, its own canon of scripture, its own theological framework, and its own place within the broader cultural landscape is the story of the second and third centuries CE. It is the story of how Christianity became Christianity — how the movement found the institutional forms that would carry it across fifteen centuries and every continent on earth.

sections.title: Key players
body: The church fathers — the theologians and bishops who shaped Christian thought and practice in the second and third centuries — were the primary architects of this institutional and intellectual development. They were not a homogeneous group. They wrote in different languages, addressed different audiences, and held different views on many questions. But together they produced the theological tradition that established the parameters of mainstream Christian belief and that gave the movement the intellectual resources to engage with the broader Greco-Roman culture from a position of confidence rather than defensiveness.

Ignatius of Antioch, who was martyred in Rome around 108 CE, is one of the earliest of these figures, and his letters — written on his journey to martyrdom — provide the first clear articulation of the threefold ministry of bishop, presbyter, and deacon as the essential structure of the Christian community. For Ignatius, the bishop was not merely an administrator but the embodiment of the community's unity, the guarantor of its connection to the apostolic tradition, and the center around which authentic Christian life was organized. His insistence on the authority of the bishop was a response to the docetic tendency he saw in some communities — the belief that Jesus had not truly become human, that his suffering had been only apparent rather than real — and his letters argue that the physical reality of Jesus's incarnation and the physical reality of the Eucharist were inseparable from each other and from the unity of the community under its bishop.

Irenaeus of Lyons, writing in the late second century, was the most systematic theologian of the pre-Nicene period and the most important defender of what he called the apostolic tradition against the various forms of Gnosticism that were competing for Christian allegiance in his time. Gnosticism — a term covering a diverse range of movements that combined Christian elements with philosophical and mythological traditions from various sources — offered an alternative interpretation of Jesus and of salvation that was, in Irenaeus's view, a fundamental distortion of the Gospel. Gnostic teachers typically distinguished between a lower, ignorant creator god — whom they identified with the God of the Hebrew Bible — and a higher, transcendent divine reality revealed by Jesus. They devalued material creation and the body, understood salvation as the liberation of a divine spark from its imprisonment in matter, and claimed access to secret teachings of Jesus unavailable to ordinary believers. Irenaeus's response was threefold: he appealed to the canon of scripture, the rule of faith, and the succession of bishops as the guarantee of continuity with the apostolic tradition.

sections.title: What happened
body: The second and third centuries were a period of intense theological controversy in which the most fundamental questions about Christian belief were contested, refined, and gradually settled. Who was Jesus? How was he related to God? What was the significance of his death? How did salvation work? What was the relationship between the God of the Hebrew Bible and the God revealed by Jesus? These questions generated an enormous body of theological writing, triggered fierce polemics between competing movements, and eventually produced the creedal formulations that defined mainstream Christian belief for all subsequent generations.

The Arian controversy of the early fourth century brought these questions to a crisis point. Arius, a priest in Alexandria, argued that the Son of God — the divine being who had become incarnate in Jesus — was a created being, brought into existence by the Father before the creation of the world. The Son was therefore subordinate to the Father and was not eternal in the same sense that the Father was eternal. Arius summarized his position in the slogan 'there was a time when he was not.' His opponent Athanasius, also of Alexandria, argued that Arius's position undermined the entire logic of salvation: if the Son was a created being and not fully divine, then the incarnation was not truly God entering into human flesh and the salvation it accomplished was not truly God's own act of redemption.

Constantine, newly converted and deeply committed to using Christianity as a unifying force within his empire, convened the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE to resolve the controversy. The council produced the Nicene Creed — a statement of faith that declared the Son to be 'of the same substance' as the Father, using a Greek philosophical term, homoousios, that had not previously been used in Christian scripture or theology. The creed was a landmark achievement, but it did not immediately end the controversy — Arianism continued to have significant support, particularly in the eastern empire and among the Germanic peoples who converted to Christianity over the following century — and the theological debates of the fourth and fifth centuries would continue to refine and elaborate the understanding of Christ's nature that Nicaea had initiated.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The institutional developments of the second and third centuries mattered because they gave Christianity the structural durability to survive the pressures it faced. The development of the episcopate — the system of bishops governing defined geographic areas — provided the movement with a leadership structure that was both flexible enough to adapt to local conditions and connected enough across geographic distances to maintain a sense of shared identity. The development of the canon gave the movement a fixed textual foundation that could be appealed to in disputes and that connected every generation of Christians to the testimony of the first witnesses. The development of the creeds gave the movement a public, memorable summary of its core convictions that could be used in baptismal instruction, in worship, and in the defense of orthodoxy against heterodox alternatives.

These institutional developments also, inevitably, involved losses. The diversity of early Christianity — the range of theological perspectives, communal practices, and interpretations of Jesus that had coexisted in the movement's first century — was progressively narrowed as the boundaries of orthodoxy were drawn more clearly. Movements that had been part of the Christian conversation in the second century — various forms of Gnosticism, Marcionism, Montanism — were defined as heresies and excluded. Texts that had been read as almost scriptural in some communities were relegated to a secondary status or lost altogether. The process of institutional consolidation that made Christianity durable also made it less open, less experimental, and less tolerant of the theological creativity that had characterized its earliest period.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The Edict of Milan in 313 CE, issued by Constantine and his co-emperor Licinius, granted Christians full legal toleration and marked the definitive end of the era of Roman persecution. Within a generation, Christianity had moved from being a tolerated minority religion to being the favored religion of the empire, and within another generation — under Theodosius I, who declared Nicene Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire in 380 CE — it had become legally mandatory for all Roman citizens. This transformation had enormous consequences. The church now had access to imperial resources, imperial prestige, and imperial enforcement mechanisms that it had never previously had. It could build basilicas, fund theological education, and convene councils with imperial sponsorship. It could also, for the first time, use the coercive power of the state against those it defined as heretics — a development whose long-term consequences for the relationship between Christianity and human freedom would be deeply troubled.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The Christianity that emerged from the first four centuries of its history — with its canon of scripture, its episcopal structure, its creeds, its liturgical practices, and its theological tradition — is recognizably the ancestor of every form of Christianity that exists today. The Orthodox churches, the Catholic Church, and the Protestant denominations that emerged from the Reformation of the sixteenth century all claim continuity with the tradition that was formed in these early centuries, even as they interpret and apply that tradition in very different ways. The Nicene Creed is still recited in Sunday worship by more than a billion Christians. The New Testament canon established in the fourth century is still the authoritative scripture of every Christian community. The theological vocabulary developed by the church fathers — incarnation, Trinity, grace, redemption — still provides the framework within which Christian theology is conducted.

What this topic has traced, from the world of Second Temple Judaism through the life of Jesus, the resurrection faith, the Jerusalem community, Paul's mission, the tensions with Judaism, the Roman context, the formation of scripture, and the consolidation of institutions, is the story of one of the most consequential developments in human history. A movement that began with a small group of Jewish disciples in a provincial corner of the Roman Empire became, within three centuries, the dominant religion of Western civilization and has since become the largest religion on earth. Understanding how that happened — what convictions drove it, what obstacles it faced, what compromises it made, and what it gained and lost along the way — is essential to understanding the world we live in, the culture we have inherited, and the ongoing significance of the questions that the life and death of Jesus of Nazareth first posed two thousand years ago.
